cataloging_subject.label: The Mirror of the Blessed Life of Jesus Christ
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The complete Powell/Brasenose beta-1 version of Nicholas Love's Middle English prose translation, adaptation, and compilation of material from the pseudo-Bonaventuran Meditationes vitae Christi: the Latin Attende lector witness paratext, prohemium, sixty-three chapters arranged for meditation across seven days, and the integrated Eucharistic treatise and final prayer conventionally numbered chapter 64. The cataloged work excludes Powell's 1908 title page, Introductory Note, table of contents, and word-list, while the linked lineage manifest records those edition and encoding boundaries. It is not a claim to one universal final authorial form, the Latin Meditationes itself, a generic life of Christ, or every item in the Brasenose manuscript.

catalog_summary.title: The Mirror of the Blessed Life of Jesus Christ
catalog_summary.author: Nicholas Love
catalog_summary.date: Translated and adapted before or around 1410
catalog_summary.region: England; exact place of composition or translation not securely established

titles.value: The Mirror of the Blessed Life of Jesus Christ
notes: Modern preferred form used by current scholarship; historical spelling is retained as an alternate title.

titles.value: The Mirrour of the Blessed Lyf of Jesu Christ
notes: Powell's title-page form, normalized only by adding the initial article used on that title page.
Latin title form preserved in the explicit and witness descriptions; it must not be confused with the language of the whole work.

agents.name: Nicholas Love
alternate_names: Nicholas Love, Prior of Mount Grace
notes: The Brasenose colophon and other early witnesses identify Love as the translator; he also adapts and compiles material beyond a literal rendering of the Latin source.

scope.description: Authorship of the resulting Middle English Mirror as a translated, adapted, and compiled work

notes: Whole-work authorship describes Love's resulting Middle English work; the separate responsibilities below preserve his translator, adapter, and compiler functions and do not assign his source-derived passages to Pseudo-Bonaventure as authorship of this work.

scope.description: Middle English rendering of selected and reorganized material from the Meditationes vitae Christi
Selection, condensation, reordering, and expansion of the pseudo-Bonaventuran source, including anti-Lollard and Eucharistic material
Compilation of translated source matter, scriptural and patristic authorities, original connective material, and the Eucharistic treatise
The Brasenose-based 1908 source edition and its modern editorial framing, not authorship of the medieval work

assertion.notes: The terminal year follows the traditional presentation/approval chronology; scholarship cautions that the text circulated in more than one early authorial form.

notes: The normalized upper bound is not a claim that every revision or the transmitted Memorandum was completed in 1410.

Love's office at Mount Grace is an association, not sufficient evidence for an exact place of translation; the Brasenose scribe's dialect and manuscript production place are also separate witness-level claims.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The XML header's lone LANGUAGE value, Latin, is erroneous as a whole-work description.

scope.description: Rubrics, quotations, chapter apparatus, the Attende note, explicit, and colophon embedded within the predominantly Middle English work

form_statements.description: A predominantly prose devotional translation and adaptation. Chapter 3 ends with Love's brief rhyming Ave Maria meditation, including the extra beta-1 'Gete us' rhyme; this selected mixed passage is recorded at part and extent level without changing the whole-work discovery form.

scope.description: Especially the Last Supper chapter and the concluding treatise and prayer
Love's orthodox polemic concerning confession, Eucharistic doctrine, and obedience to ecclesiastical teaching
The Annunciation and Nativity, Mary's compassion at the Passion, Holy Saturday, and the first Resurrection appearance

summaries.value: Nicholas Love's Middle English Mirror guides readers through imaginative meditation on Christ's life from the heavenly counsel before the Incarnation through Nativity, ministry, Passion, Resurrection, Ascension, and Pentecost. Recasting and condensing the pseudo-Bonaventuran Meditationes vitae Christi into sixty-three chapters for seven days of reading, Love adds extensive vernacular explanation, devotional direction, and orthodox polemic. A concluding Eucharistic treatise and prayer defend the Real Presence and make the sacrament the culminating memorial of Christ's earthly life.

The cataloged Powell/Brasenose beta-1 version includes the medieval Latin Attende lector witness paratext, prohemium, seven day-sections, sixty-three narrative and expository chapters, the Eucharistic treatise conventionally numbered chapter 64, and its final prayer. The U-M XML additionally retains Powell's title page, Introductory Note, and table of contents, but omits Powell's pp. 325-330 word-list and nearly all manuscript marginalia. The editorial front and encoding choices are not treated as medieval authorship, and completeness is not a claim to one universal final authorial form.

The XML's LANGUAGE value Latin is a header defect: the work is predominantly Middle English prose with embedded Latin. Powell's source is a beta-version text based on Brasenose College MS 9 and collated with two other witnesses; its spellings, punctuation, chapter structure, and omissions therefore describe an edited transmission layer rather than a single authorial original.

extent.description: Sixty-four numbered items: sixty-two prose-only DIV2 chapters plus mixed chapter 3 are organized under seven day-sections, and the top-level prose Eucharistic treatise is conventionally numbered chapter 64, yielding 63 prose-only and one mixed numbered item. Eleven modeled content parts comprise the Latin Attende lector witness paratext, prohemium, seven days, treatise, and nested final prayer. Chapter 3 contains one brief authorial rhyme group, but Powell and the XML do not preserve lineation securely enough for a verse-line count; the XML's P-only encoding is not evidence that the rhyme is prose. Three and two DIV3 subdivisions respectively refine chapters 3 and 33 without increasing the chapter total. Counts exclude Powell's title page, Introductory Note, table of contents, and word-list.

content_parts.label: Attende lector
content_parts.summary: A Latin instruction to the reader explains the manuscript's marginal N and B letters as distinguishing Love's translator-compiler material from passages derived from the work attributed to Bonaventure.
notes: Although encoded within FRONT and printed after Powell's contents, this is medieval witness paratext and belongs to the cataloged textual unit.

content_parts.label: Prohemium
content_parts.summary: Love explains the work's use for simple devout readers, its imaginative method, its relation to the Latin Meditationes, and the recommended weekly and liturgical reading pattern.

content_parts.label: Monday: Incarnation and early life
content_parts.summary: Nine chapters move from the heavenly counsel and Mary's life through the Incarnation, Annunciation, Visitation, Nativity, Circumcision, Epiphany, and Purification.
notes: Chapter 3 has three encoded subparts; these do not increase the canonical chapter count.

Its final Ave Maria meditation contains Love's authorial rhyme, with the additional 'Gete us' lines characteristic of the beta-1 version. Powell and the XML run the passage as prose, so one verse group is grounded but a line count is not.

content_parts.label: Tuesday: Childhood and preparation for ministry
content_parts.summary: Five chapters treat the flight into and return from Egypt, Christ in the Temple, his hidden life, and his Baptism and withdrawal into the desert.

content_parts.label: Wednesday: Temptation, teaching, and early ministry
content_parts.summary: Ten chapters cover Christ's fast and temptations, gathering of disciples, Cana, the Sermon on the Mount, healings, Mary Magdalene's conversion, and controversies with Pharisees.

content_parts.label: Thursday: Later ministry and the Last Supper
content_parts.summary: Fifteen chapters move through miracles, active and contemplative life, Lazarus, the entry into Jerusalem, and the Last Supper, ending at the threshold of the Passion.
notes: The encoding combines two chapter-33 headings as subparts of one chapter.

content_parts.label: Friday: Passion and burial
content_parts.summary: Nine chapters guide compassionate meditation from Christ's prayer and arrest through trials, Crucifixion, death, deposition, burial, and the disciples' and Mary's grief.

content_parts.label: Saturday: Mourning and the descent into hell
content_parts.summary: One chapter depicts Mary, the apostles, and the women in mourning and preparation, then contemplates Christ's descent to the fathers in hell.

content_parts.label: Sunday: Resurrection, Ascension, and Pentecost
content_parts.summary: Fourteen chapters treat the Resurrection and appearances, the Ascension, and the descent of the Holy Spirit, closing the Speculum vitae Christi proper.

content_parts.label: A Short Treatise of the Sacrament of Christ's Blessed Body
content_parts.summary: The treatise expounds the Real Presence, obedience to Church teaching, Eucharistic miracles, and anti-Lollard polemic, making the sacrament a memorial and culmination of Christ's life.
notes: The table treats this integrated work as chapter 64; the XML gives it a top-level treatise division rather than a DIV2 chapter.

content_parts.label: Final Eucharistic prayer
content_parts.summary: A prayer for devout recitation before the sacrament asks Christ for healing, grace, virtue, amendment of life, and final union with him.

locators.value: LoveMirrour:12, final two P elements; pp. 323-324

related_works.title: Meditationes vitae Christi
notes: The Latin devotional work is traditionally attributed to Bonaventure but treated here as anonymous/pseudo-Bonaventuran. Love condenses, reorganizes, translates, and supplements it; no particular surviving Latin physical exemplar is asserted.

evidence.summary: Supplies the electronic title and identifiers, Powell source statement, Middle English text, embedded Latin, authorship colophon, encoding history, medieval Attende lector paratext, retained editorial front matter, omitted marginalia statement, and the complete encoded Powell/Brasenose beta-1 structure.

evidence.citation: Element, hierarchy, heading, and page-milestone inspection of LoveMirrour.xml.
evidence.locator: 12 DIV1, 63 DIV2, 5 DIV3, 399 P, and 327 PB elements
evidence.summary: Confirms Powell's retained title page, Introductory Note, and table of contents; the separate medieval Attende lector paratext; the prohemium; seven day-sections containing 9, 5, 10, 15, 9, 1, and 14 chapters; the top-level Eucharistic treatise; and the final prayer. It also establishes the page ranges and the erroneous whole-work LANGUAGE value.

evidence.citation: Nicholas Love, The Mirrour of the Blessed Lyf of Jesu Christ, ed. Lawrence F. Powell (Oxford: Clarendon Press; London: Henry Frowde, 1908), PIMS/University of Toronto scan.
evidence.locator: title page; Introductory Note pp. iii-iv; contents pp. [1]-[6]; work pp. 7-324; word-list pp. 325-330
evidence.summary: Complete scan of the immediate edition confirming the exact title, edition boundary, Brasenose base, two collated witnesses, editorial practices, the complete Powell/Brasenose beta-1 version, and the word-list omitted from the XML.

evidence.citation: Ian Johnson, 'Theoretical and Pragmatic Dialogics in and through Nicholas Love's Mirror of the Blessed Life of Jesus Christ,' English 67.257 (2018): 97-118, accepted author manuscript.
evidence.locator: abstract and discussion of translation, compilation, source voices, anti-Lollard polemic, and Eucharistic additions
evidence.summary: Peer-reviewed scholarship supporting the before-1410 date, pseudo-Bonaventuran source, translation-and-adaptation boundary, compilation model, and Love's anti-Lollard and Eucharistic additions.
notes: The cached provider PDF is a 32-page accepted manuscript; qpdf reports recoverable zero-offset warnings but succeeds, and all pages extract without repair.

evidence.citation: Allan Westphall and David Falls, 'Mirror of the Blessed Life of Jesus Christ,' Geographies of Orthodoxy textual profile, rev. 1 June 2010.
evidence.locator: Approximate Date, Sources, Provenance; Contents; Textual Authority; Theology
evidence.summary: Scholarly profile grounded in Sargent's full critical edition, supporting authorship, source transformation, sixty-four-chapter and seven-day organization, marginal-note scheme, Eucharistic treatise, anti-Lollard interventions, and caution about a single final authorial form.

evidence.citation: Geographies of Orthodoxy, 'The textual affiliations of the witnesses to the Mirror' and 'The Meditation on the Ave Maria,' based on Michael G. Sargent's critical edition.
evidence.locator: The Meditation on the Ave Maria; beta-1 text and the additional 'Gete us' rhyme
evidence.summary: Prints Love's rhyming chapter-3 Ave Maria meditation, identifies the additional 'Gete us these virtues' rhyme as beta-1 material, and distinguishes the beta, alpha, and gamma witness affiliations. It grounds one mixed chapter and one verse group but does not supply stable lineation for Powell's running-prose presentation.

evidence.citation: Ryan Perry, description of Oxford, Brasenose College MS 9, Geographies of Orthodoxy, rev. 1 June 2010.
evidence.locator: incipit, colophon, explicit, production date, foliation, scribal description, and Sargent grouping
evidence.summary: Identifies the early-fifteenth-century Brasenose witness, Love colophon, table and prohemium loci, explicit at fol. 150v, beta-1 grouping and Bc siglum, and fragmentary Middle English Meditationes material removed from the codex.

evidence.citation: General Corpus lineage manifest for LoveMirrour.
evidence.summary: Binds the catalog record to Powell's edition, the U-M encoding, the local artifact, the three named source witnesses, and the Latin source work while preserving access and rights by layer.

open_questions.question: How should the chronology and exact contents of Love's early beta form and later alpha revision be dated at passage level?
next_steps: Consult Sargent's full critical introduction and apparatus before assigning individual additions or the Eucharistic treatise to a precise revision event.
notes: The Brasenose-based Powell text represents beta-1, while the work survives in multiple early forms; a single 1410 completion event would overstate the evidence.

open_questions.question: Which physical Latin exemplar or exemplars did Love actually use?
next_steps: Evaluate the proposed Ripon Cathedral MS 6 connection and reading-level comparisons in current scholarship; retain only the abstract Meditationes source relation until physical use is established.

open_questions.question: Can the place or places of translation, compilation, and revision be established more narrowly than England?
next_steps: Consult biographical and textual scholarship that distinguishes Love's Mount Grace office from evidence for where each textual phase was produced.

open_questions.question: Was the XML header's whole-work LANGUAGE value Latin introduced by conversion error, inherited metadata, or a misunderstood source-language field?
next_steps: Ask U-M for production metadata if the defect's history matters; continue faceting the inspected work as Middle English with embedded Latin.

notes: The transmitted Arundel Memorandum is historical evidence within the manuscript tradition, not treated here as a surviving administrative approval record or as proof that one final form was fixed in 1410.

The Brasenose scribe's western Buckinghamshire dialect localization and the other source witnesses' dates belong to their material transmission layers, not to the abstract work's composition place or date.

Powell's twenty-three-manuscript count and late-fifteenth-century dating of both collated witnesses are retained as historical edition claims but superseded for current description by later witness scholarship and institutional cataloging.
